paper_metadata.title: When worry about climate change leads to climate action: How values, worry and personal responsibility relate to various climate actions
paper_metadata.authors: Thijs Bouman; Mark Verschoor; Casper J. Albers; Gisela Böhm; Stephen D. Fisher; Wouter Poortinga; Lorraine Whitmarsh; Linda Steg
paper_metadata.empirical_scope_or_sample: European Social Survey Round 8 climate change and energy module; N=44,387 respondents
exact_tax_terms_used: increasing taxes on fossil fuels, such as oil, gas and coal
paper_metadata.carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Uses ESS Round 8 to model determinants of support for increasing taxes on fossil fuels (oil, gas, coal) across 23 countries; psychological variables include biospheric values, climate worry, and personal responsibility.

determinants.definition: Endorsement of biospheric values (strongly believing people should care for nature; caring for the environment).
determinants.mechanism: Values increase engagement with climate-relevant concerns and mitigation, but their association with fossil-fuel-tax support is small and varies by country; e.g., negative in Hungary.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for “increasing taxes on fossil fuels, such as oil, gas and coal” (ESS8 item: tax fossil fuels).
determinants.outcome_variable: Support for tax on fossil fuels (“increasing taxes on fossil fuels, such as oil, gas and coal”)
determinants.evidence_quote: Table 1 (Tax fossil fuels): biospheric values β=0.03 [.02,.04]; authors note biospheric values were strongly negatively related to fossil-fuel-tax support in Hungary.
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Multivariate: Tax fossil fuels R²=.06; biospheric values β̂=0.03 (95% CI [.02, .04]) controlling for worry and personal responsibility.
determinants.notes: Overall effect is positive but the relationship is heterogeneous; one country (Hungary) shows a notably negative association.

determinants.definition: Self-reported worry about climate change (5-point scale).
determinants.mechanism: Climate worry reflects emotionally engaged threat appraisal; it is associated with stronger policy support for fossil-fuel taxes, both directly and partly via increased personal responsibility.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for “increasing taxes on fossil fuels, such as oil, gas and coal” (ESS8 item: tax fossil fuels).
determinants.outcome_variable: Support for tax on fossil fuels (“increasing taxes on fossil fuels, such as oil, gas and coal”)
determinants.evidence_quote: Abstract: worry was “both directly and indirectly, via feelings of personal responsibility… associated with climate policy support”; Table 1 shows worry β=0.10 [.09,.11] for Tax fossil fuels.
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Multivariate: Tax fossil fuels R²=.06; worry about CC β̂=0.10 (95% CI [.09, .11]) controlling for biospheric values and personal responsibility.
determinants.notes: Direct positive association with fossil-fuel-tax support is present in the multivariate model; mediation via personal responsibility is discussed qualitatively.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Feelings of personal responsibility to reduce climate change
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Personal responsibility to reduce climate change
determinants.definition: Self-reported personal responsibility to try to reduce climate change (0–10 scale).
determinants.mechanism: Norm activation: perceiving personal responsibility motivates climate action and strengthens support for society-wide measures like fossil-fuel taxation.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for “increasing taxes on fossil fuels, such as oil, gas and coal” (ESS8 item: tax fossil fuels).
determinants.outcome_variable: Support for tax on fossil fuels (“increasing taxes on fossil fuels, such as oil, gas and coal”)
determinants.evidence_quote: Table 1 (Tax fossil fuels): personal responsibility β=0.17 [.16,.18].
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Multivariate: Tax fossil fuels R²=.06; personal responsibility β̂=0.17 (95% CI [.16, .18]) controlling for worry and biospheric values.
determinants.notes: The paper also argues worry increases policy support partly by increasing feelings of personal responsibility.

exclusions_or_uncertainties.issue: Other “climate policy support” items not extracted
exclusions_or_uncertainties.reason: The paper’s climate policy support scale includes non-tax policies (renewables subsidies; bans on least energy-efficient appliances). Determinants for those are outside the carbon/fuel-tax focus.
